title: Store and print command output
prompt: Write a shell script snippet that stores today's date in a variable `TODAY` and prints "Today is: <date>".

correctAnswer.solution:
TODAY=$(date +%F)
echo "Today is: $TODAY"

explanation: `$(command)` is command substitution — it runs `date` and captures stdout into the variable. `+%F` formats the date as YYYY-MM-DD. Double quotes let `$TODAY` expand inside the string.

title: Check if a file exists
prompt: Write an if-statement that prints "Found" if `/etc/app/config.yml` exists, otherwise prints "Missing".

correctAnswer.solution:
if [ -f /etc/app/config.yml ]; then
  echo "Found"
else
  echo "Missing"
fi

explanation: `-f` is a test operator that is true when the path exists and is a regular file (not a directory). The `[ ]` syntax is the POSIX `test` command.

title: Print numbers 1 to 5
prompt: Write a for-loop that prints the numbers 1 through 5, one per line.

correctAnswer.solution:
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
  echo $i
done

explanation: A `for ... in` loop iterates over the listed words (here, the literal numbers). `$i` holds the current value on each iteration. `seq 1 5` is an equally valid alternative to the literal list.

title: Read a script argument
prompt: Write a script that prints "Hello, <name>" where `<name>` is the first argument passed to the script, defaulting to "World" if no argument is given.

correctAnswer.solution:
NAME=${1:-World}
echo "Hello, $NAME"

explanation: `$1` is the first positional argument. `${1:-World}` uses parameter expansion to substitute the default "World" only when `$1` is unset or empty, without needing a separate if-check.

title: Compare two strings
prompt: Write an if-statement that prints "Match" if variable `ENV` equals "production", otherwise prints "Not production".

correctAnswer.solution:
if [ "$ENV" = "production" ]; then
  echo "Match"
else
  echo "Not production"
fi

explanation: Quoting `"$ENV"` prevents word-splitting/glob errors if the variable is empty or contains spaces. `=` (a single equals sign) is the POSIX string-equality test inside `[ ]`.

title: Check the last command's success
prompt: Write a script snippet that runs `ping -c1 example.com` and prints "UP" if it succeeded, or "DOWN" if it failed.

correctAnswer.solution:
if ping -c1 example.com > /dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo "UP"
else
  echo "DOWN"
fi

explanation: Placing the command directly in the `if` uses its exit status (0 = success) as the condition — no need for a separate `$?` check. Redirecting stdout and stderr to `/dev/null` keeps the ping output from cluttering the script's output.

title: Reusable logging function
prompt: Write a shell function `log()` that prefixes any message passed to it with the current timestamp, e.g. `log "Backup started"` prints `[2026-07-02 10:15:00] Backup started`.
explanation: Functions are defined with `name() { ... }`. Inside, `$1` refers to the function's first argument (separate from the script's own `$1`). This pattern is the backbone of consistent logging across ops scripts.

title: Loop over an array of hosts
prompt: Given `HOSTS=(web1 web2 db1)`, write a loop that pings each host once and prints "<host>: reachable" or "<host>: unreachable".

correctAnswer.solution:
HOSTS=(web1 web2 db1)
for host in "${HOSTS[@]}"; do
  if ping -c1 "$host" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "$host: reachable"
  else
    echo "$host: unreachable"
  fi
done

explanation: `"${HOSTS[@]}"` expands each array element as a separate, correctly-quoted word — critical if any hostname ever contains special characters. Looping directly over the array avoids manual index bookkeeping.

title: Fail fast on any error
prompt: Add the standard safety header to a deployment script so that it exits immediately if any command fails, if an unset variable is referenced, or if any command in a pipeline fails.

correctAnswer.solution:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

explanation: `-e` exits on any non-zero exit status, `-u` treats unset variables as an error instead of silently expanding to empty, and `-o pipefail` makes a pipeline's exit status the first non-zero one instead of just the last command's — without it, `false | true` would report success.

title: Parse named command-line flags
prompt: Write a script snippet that reads `--env=<value>` from its arguments and stores `<value>` in a variable `ENV`.

correctAnswer.solution:
for arg in "$@"; do
  case $arg in
    --env=*)
      ENV="${arg#*=}"
      ;;
  esac
done
echo "ENV=$ENV"

explanation: `"$@"` expands all arguments individually. The `case` pattern `--env=*` matches any argument starting with `--env=`, and `${arg#*=}` strips everything up to and including the first `=`, leaving just the value.

title: Process a file line by line
prompt: Write a loop that reads `servers.txt` (one hostname per line) and echoes "Checking: <hostname>" for each line, correctly handling a file with no trailing newline.

correctAnswer.solution:
while IFS= read -r line || [ -n "$line" ]; do
  echo "Checking: $line"
done < servers.txt

explanation: `IFS=` preserves leading/trailing whitespace, `-r` stops backslashes from being interpreted as escapes, and `|| [ -n "$line" ]` ensures the final line is still processed even if the file doesn't end with a newline (otherwise `read` would return failure and skip it).

title: Retry a flaky command
prompt: Write a script snippet that retries `curl -sf https://api.example.com/health` up to 3 times with a 5-second pause between attempts, exiting with an error if all attempts fail.

correctAnswer.solution:
for attempt in 1 2 3; do
  if curl -sf https://api.example.com/health; then
    exit 0
  fi
  echo "Attempt $attempt failed, retrying..."
  sleep 5
done
echo "All attempts failed" >&2
exit 1

explanation: `-f` makes curl fail (non-zero exit) on HTTP error codes, which is what the loop condition checks. Sending the final failure message to `>&2` (stderr) is standard practice for error output so it doesn't pollute stdout that other tools might parse.

title: Guarantee cleanup on exit
prompt: Write a script that creates a temp file, and guarantees the temp file is deleted whether the script exits normally, is interrupted (Ctrl+C), or errors out.

correctAnswer.solution:
#!/bin/bash
TMPFILE=$(mktemp)
trap 'rm -f "$TMPFILE"' EXIT

# ... use $TMPFILE ...
echo "data" > "$TMPFILE"

explanation: `trap ... EXIT` registers a cleanup command that bash runs on any script exit path — normal completion, `exit`, an uncaught error under `set -e`, or a signal like SIGINT — making it far more reliable than manually adding `rm` before every exit point.

title: Compare output of two commands
prompt: Write a command that diffs the sorted contents of `list1.txt` and `list2.txt` without creating any intermediate temp files.

correctAnswer.solution: diff <(sort list1.txt) <(sort list2.txt)

explanation: `<(command)` is process substitution — bash runs the command and exposes its output as a file-like path (e.g. `/dev/fd/63`) that `diff` can read directly, avoiding the need to manually write sorted output to temp files first.

title: Run tasks in parallel and wait
prompt: Write a script that runs three backup functions (`backup_db`, `backup_files`, `backup_config`) concurrently and only continues once all three finish, failing the script if any of them failed.

correctAnswer.solution:
backup_db & pid1=$!
backup_files & pid2=$!
backup_config & pid3=$!

wait $pid1 $pid2 $pid3
status=$?
if [ $status -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "One or more backups failed" >&2
  exit 1
fi

explanation: `&` backgrounds each function call; `$!` captures the PID of the most recently backgrounded process so it can be waited on individually. `wait` blocks until the listed PIDs finish and returns the exit status of the last one waited on, which is checked to decide overall success.

title: Basic integer arithmetic
prompt: Write a shell snippet that adds two numbers stored in variables `A=5` and `B=3` and prints the result.
explanation: `$((...))` is arithmetic expansion — it evaluates the enclosed expression as integer math and substitutes the numeric result, without needing `expr` or external tools.

title: Write multi-line text to a file
prompt: Write a script snippet that writes a 2-line message into `notice.txt` using a heredoc.

correctAnswer.solution:
cat << 'EOF' > notice.txt
Maintenance window tonight.
Expect brief downtime.
EOF

explanation: A heredoc (`<< 'EOF' ... EOF`) lets you write multi-line literal text inline in a script; quoting `'EOF'` prevents variable expansion inside the block, useful when the text itself might contain `$` characters you don't want interpreted.

title: Use a command's output as a filename
prompt: Write a snippet that creates a backup file named `backup-<today's date>.tar.gz`, e.g. `backup-2026-07-02.tar.gz`.

correctAnswer.solution: tar -czf "backup-$(date +%F).tar.gz" /data

explanation: `$(date +%F)` runs inside the double-quoted string and is replaced with today's date before the filename is used, letting you build dynamic filenames in one line.

title: Check if a variable is empty
prompt: Write an if-statement that prints "Missing DB_HOST" if the environment variable `DB_HOST` is unset or empty.

correctAnswer.solution:
if [ -z "$DB_HOST" ]; then
  echo "Missing DB_HOST"
fi

explanation: `-z` is true when the string is empty (zero length). Quoting `"$DB_HOST"` is important — without quotes, an unset variable could make the test syntactically break instead of evaluating cleanly.

title: Combine two conditions with AND
prompt: Write an if-statement that prints "Ready" only if both `/opt/app/bin` exists AND is executable.

correctAnswer.solution:
if [ -e /opt/app/bin ] && [ -x /opt/app/bin ]; then
  echo "Ready"
fi

explanation: `&&` between two `[ ]` tests only runs (and requires success of) the second test if the first succeeds — the standard way to chain multiple conditions in POSIX shell without a single compound expression.

title: Branch on a value with case
prompt: Write a case statement that prints "Linux", "Mac", or "Unknown OS" based on a variable `OS_TYPE` containing 'linux', 'darwin', or anything else.
explanation: `case` is cleaner than a chain of `if/elif` for matching one variable against several fixed values; `*)` acts as the catch-all default, matching anything not caught by an earlier pattern.

title: Count words matching a pattern via pipeline
prompt: Write a pipeline that counts how many lines in `deploy.log` contain the word "FAILED".

correctAnswer.solution: grep -c 'FAILED' deploy.log

explanation: `grep -c` counts matching lines directly instead of needing to pipe through `wc -l` separately — a small but common shortcut once you know it exists.

title: Process a variable number of arguments
prompt: Write a script that prints each argument passed to it on its own line, prefixed with its position number, without knowing in advance how many arguments there are.

correctAnswer.solution:
i=1
for arg in "$@"; do
  echo "$i: $arg"
  i=$((i + 1))
done

explanation: Looping over `"$@"` handles any number of arguments safely (each expands as its own quoted word), while a manually incremented counter `i` tracks position — an alternative using `shift` in a `while [ $# -gt 0 ]` loop is equally valid.

title: Avoid polluting global scope in a function
prompt: Write a function `calculate_total()` that uses a local variable for its intermediate sum so it doesn't leak into or overwrite any global variable also named `total`.

correctAnswer.solution:
calculate_total() {
  local total=0
  for n in "$@"; do
    total=$((total + n))
  done
  echo $total
}

explanation: `local` scopes a variable to the function it's declared in; without it, `total` inside the function would silently overwrite any global variable of the same name, a common source of bugs in longer scripts.

title: Handle SIGTERM gracefully
prompt: Write a long-running script skeleton that, when it receives SIGTERM, prints "Shutting down gracefully..." and exits cleanly instead of dying immediately.

correctAnswer.solution:
cleanup() {
  echo "Shutting down gracefully..."
  exit 0
}
trap cleanup SIGTERM

while true; do
  sleep 1
done

explanation: `trap cleanup SIGTERM` registers a handler function to run when the process receives that specific signal (e.g. from `kill` or `docker stop`), letting the script finish in-flight work or clean up resources instead of being killed mid-operation.

title: Extract a file extension
prompt: Write a snippet that extracts the extension from a filename stored in `FILE="report.final.csv"`, printing `csv`.

correctAnswer.solution:
FILE="report.final.csv"
echo "${FILE##*.}"

explanation: `${FILE##*.}` strips the longest match of `*.` from the front of the string, leaving only what's after the last dot — the standard parameter-expansion idiom for extracting a file extension without calling an external `basename`/`sed` process.

title: Generate a config file from a template
prompt: Write a script snippet that generates `app.conf` from variables `PORT=8080` and `ENV=production`, embedding their values.
explanation: Using an unquoted `EOF` delimiter (unlike the earlier quoted `'EOF'` example) allows variable expansion inside the heredoc body, so `${PORT}` and `${ENV}` are substituted with their actual values as the file is written.

title: Coordinate two processes with a FIFO
prompt: Write a script that creates a named pipe, has one background process write lines to it, and a foreground process read and print them.

correctAnswer.solution:
mkfifo /tmp/mypipe

(
  echo "line one"
  sleep 1
  echo "line two"
) > /tmp/mypipe &

while read -r line < /tmp/mypipe; do
  echo "Received: $line"
done

rm /tmp/mypipe

explanation: `mkfifo` creates a named pipe (FIFO) — a filesystem entry that behaves like an in-memory pipe but can be opened by unrelated processes by path, unlike an anonymous `|` pipe which only connects processes in the same pipeline.

title: Trace script execution for debugging
prompt: Write the way you would run a script `deploy.sh` to print every command it executes, with variable values already substituted, before running it — for debugging a failure in CI.

correctAnswer.solution:
bash -x deploy.sh

# or, to trace just one section inside the script itself:
set -x
# ... suspect code ...
set +x

explanation: `bash -x` (or `set -x` inline) prints each command to stderr, prefixed with `+`, after variable and command substitution has been applied — showing exactly what actually ran rather than the literal source, which is invaluable for CI failures that don't reproduce locally.

title: Make a provisioning script safe to re-run
prompt: Write a script snippet that creates a system user `appuser` and a directory `/opt/app`, safely re-runnable without erroring if either already exists.

correctAnswer.solution:
if ! id -u appuser >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  useradd -r -s /usr/sbin/nologin appuser
fi

mkdir -p /opt/app
chown appuser:appuser /opt/app

explanation: `id -u appuser` fails (non-zero exit) if the user doesn't exist yet, so the `if !` guard only creates it when needed instead of erroring on a re-run. `mkdir -p` is already inherently idempotent — it silently succeeds if the directory already exists, unlike plain `mkdir`.
